speaker: interviewer
text: January 31st, 1997. The survivor is Wanda Lorenc. Her maiden name was Wos. I'm interviewing her. My name is Anne Bernard. The interview is taking place in Sea Cliff, New York, in the United States of America. And the interview is taking place in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: January 31st, 1997. The survivor is Wanda Lorenc. Her maiden name was Wos. I'm interviewing her. My name is Anne Bernard. The interview is taking place in Sea Cliff, New York, in the United States of America. And the interview is taking place in English. Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Wanda Paulina Lorenc.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-N-D-A P-A-U-L-I-N-A-- this is middle name. And last name is Lorenc, L-O-R-E-N-C.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wos-- W-O-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland, in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: June 22nd, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 68 and half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your parents. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name, Pawel-- it is the same as Paul-- Wos. My mother's name, Anna Wos, maiden name, Szachowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-Z-A-C-H-O-W-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two brothers, one sister. First brother is Paul Wos, also, also Paul. Sister, Irena Madjski, other brother, Odon-- George Odon Wos. And I'm last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the difference in age between all of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two or three years, because-- yes, two or three years. My older brother is eight years older as I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: What religion was practiced in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are family-- Catholic family, very religious. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's it-- practicing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do-- what do you mean by very religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: We practiced going each Sunday to the church. We try-- especially my mother was very religious. We tried to keep day, days without meat, to go to church for special occasion, and also to keep whatever was done in the church. Don't do something wrong against other. Be good, love other people. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the church you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my childhood first, we participate in masses in cathedral chur-- church in Old Town, Saint John Cathedral. And later when we moved to [? Modova ?] Street, also in Old Town, we went to [INAUDIBLE] church on [INAUDIBLE] or to church in [INAUDIBLE] where I was living later, but mostly in Warsaw to Catholic church. Each Sunday, we belongs to small organization, Catholics organization. But life, life in our families was very quiet, lot of laugh.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had small factory with knitting. He was very busy man but still sacrificing for us time for vacation, for walking, for explanation, so many things in the life. Mother some time of life, she provide the small store with [? gallantry, ?] with everything for school, dresses, some small equipment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your mother's store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna, Anna Store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of your father's factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you true, [LAUGHS] I don't remember. Probably it was Knitting, Knitting Factory. I don't remember. I was not-- too young and I didn't interested much bef--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the store located?

speaker: interviewee
text: The store was located on [INAUDIBLE] Street. But factory was in [INAUDIBLE], very close to near [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] 39. It was quite big for me in this time. I was young and small. But this factory looked very big.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father maintain the factory during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, some part-- he, he had permission because he had to give some contribution for probably gover-- German government. But I tell you true. I was not so involved in this problem. I was mostly interested in school. And I start to be very patriotical girl in this time, not thinking about pleasure or, or work, only about freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a child were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- Because I was the youngest, probably I, I was spoiled. Everybody gave me something. I had-- I was participating in school, underground school. Because you know that German government start to close all school, because they didn't want to have intelligentsia in Poland. And we-- I was in school during occupation, of course. I was in school, trade school, with my older son, George Odon Wos. But still, still this time we're-- we're thinking only about freedom for our country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we, we get into that period of time, I would like to go back to more of your earliest childhood. And if you could tell us what your earliest memories were of-- of family gathering?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very happy. I had very big, romantic thinking about [? elves ?] or something under influence of book and lullaby, [LAUGHS] which sung our mother. I told you that I was spoiled. But life in my family was very nice. Each Sunday after church, my father took us for big trip around the Warsaw to showing everything, these houses, built in this, somebody did-- explaining as, as much as possible. Probably I was a little rebellious. I preferred to have cinema or one cone of ice cream. But my father introduce us to many, many things, and very early.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would you say influenced you the most as a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it-- both of parents. She was terrific mother, terrific hostess. I remember when she prepare-- prepared for Christmastime, she made own dolls for us. And I was looking [LAUGHS] from the, from the other room what she is doing-- curious, of course. But it was really nice, happy life. My parents were working lot but still having for us lot of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any help in the house besides your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I remember, always was somebody because was four children. And I have also bad memories with one maid when I start to curse her. I don't know where, when I heard bad words. When she was bathing me, I start to curse her. [LAUGHS] But that's bad, bad memories. But anyway, always was somebody who helped my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did that upset you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This maid? Because I didn't want to be bathed. And naked and I cursed. And she clapped me in my back. I did again. But I lost. [LAUGHS] She won.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the favorite foods that your mother used to cook for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was terrific cook. I would like to be like her. Everything was so good, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What stands out in your memory?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was-- babka, big cake done in special way. Nobody do now like she was doing, very good babka. Also some stuff like sauerkraut, bigos-- probably you didn't know what is bigos. Bigos is meat and sauerkraut, and kielbasa. Is done very long time with wine and prune.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the apartment in which you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, when I was four years old, we moved to Mariensztat to apartment, but for short time. Because it was too small. And later, mostly I remember apartment only on [INAUDIBLE] Street. Otherwise, like, belongs to Old Town. And this house, this building was very large. We shared this house with priest, Greek Catholic priest. We are not Greek Catholic, but my father was like friend with this father who provided. We rented-- my father rented third floor. It was four, four rooms, big corridor, waiting room big like this one, and kitchen also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have running water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure-- running water, gas, electricity, big storage for food-- it was not so popular like now. That storage was cool. It was ice cubes inside special ice cube, like refrigerator but was differ-- different as, as is now, of course, because it was so many years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the financial circumstances of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think was very bad-- very good, very good. Because my older siblings, they went to private high schools. It was quite expensive in this time. For one month, I think it was about 100 or 120 zloty. And it was three already in high school. I was still in elementary school. And I finished four grade in elementary school before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people did your father have working for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't tell you. I know that's very nice people, but I can't tell you. My brother probably he will know much better. Because he was working, working with him, with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about your schooling. Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I was in public school [INAUDIBLE] 18-- very nice, modern, beautiful school. I was also in group of plastic and dancing, like ballet group. Now could be said ballet. We-- I was in this school with my sister and my brother four years only, very nice school. And the name of the school was Jozef Pilsudski, our president name. And also, I remember dur-- before the war, we're always together, whole family without my mother. Because she never was skating-- small and fat. And we went for skating with our aunt very close to my school. I remember it was so nice, so happy time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you skate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? Also on [INAUDIBLE] or on [INAUDIBLE]. It was many, many places for skating. But we used in this time different kind of skate-- everything removable, not like now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you skate on lakes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only or special place-- my parents wouldn't let us to go for unsafe place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the other activities that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. I mentioned you before that Catholic group for performance, something like Christmas carols or performance. Also this sport [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and vacation in the, in, in the field with my father who told us about each place, each wood, what is it. What's the name about mushrooms, what we can take, what not. It's-- it's not too much memories, because I was on-- 11 when start to be war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go on vacation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually when vacation was in school. School finished, like here, about 22nd or 23rd of June for two months, through first of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To, to the country-- I don't remember, [INAUDIBLE] or somewhere. I don't remember-- in the wood, beautiful places.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you in the family went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually, Father took us four. Sometimes Mother came for a short time or our aunt, who was single. And she was like second mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia, and last name Szachowski like maiden name of my mother. She was younger. And we loved her so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: She survive. But she passed away very young, in my opinion, of course. She was 52 when she died. She was in concentration camp. She survive-- [INAUDIBLE] bad memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite subject in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, I tell you true-- in, before the war or after? Before the war-- everything was very nice for me and easy. I had older brothers. They helped if was necessary. But I didn't like to draw. When I give money, my brother did everything for me. [LAUGHS] Usually, I had the best gra-- the best grade in school. When I finished my seven years in elementary school, it was everything on top. I was-- in this time, I was very clever. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any pets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sure. Our family loved pets. We had cat, of course, one, at least one, dogs, cana-- small birds, turtles, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] whatever was possible to keep in our house and Mother let us. Even we had duck, because it was so nice, so colorful. [LAUGHS] And we kept it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I love dogs. Still, I love. I love dogs. We had so nice. And after special school, one German Shepard was so clever. He went with small amount of money to, to buy. They knew. They knew that what my mother is reading. And she bring home this newspaper. But she was very demanding when she saw men holding ice cream. Because not like now, before the war in this area, they brought ice cream in special container. And they sell in cone for five, five [INAUDIBLE]-- you know, the small coin. When this dog saw this man, she was sitting as long as she got this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, cat was very, very, very clever. When she went from outside, she meow. Then somebody ask, who is it? Meow. [LAUGHS] She could open even faucet with cold water, very clever. But I remember one day when she went to a place in the oven, but not gas oven. Only for coal was in this time. Was very hot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this maid open and leave to, to make little cooler. She went there. Later, this maid forgot that cat is sitting. She close. And she start to cook. It was terrible noise, because it was too hot. That's memories from the childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about the neighborhood in which you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Neighborhood-- you see, mostly I remember from this [INAUDIBLE] Street. It was very quiet building, closed for keys. Nobody can go from outside. And we are very safe. But we took this third store. Behind on second and first, there were living priests. And I was still so stupid to make stupid jokes. When one priest was looking outside and I see this bald head, I try to put water on it or, of course, ringing the bell. They know that's me. But it was-- neighborhood was very nice, quiet. This-- it-- this is very nice street still. I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there many Jews that lived in that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I can't tell you. But very close to, to [INAUDIBLE] it was a area where many Jews was living. Now, [INAUDIBLE] was very close. For me, it was so nice to go with my mother to [INAUDIBLE] because so many stores. And for me, it was always nice to be in this very alive area-- many people, many jumping, buying, asking, come here. Buy something-- very nice area. My father factory was very close to, to this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family have any Jewish friends or have any Jewish relationships before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. My father was in business with Jewish people. And I think it start to be like friendship. I was in school, sitting on the same place with Jewish girl. I don't remember her name. But it was normal, not separation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was-- in my family, I tell, tell you, it was not different between a Jewish people and Polish. We spent some Easter or Passovers in our house. We went to them. They, they came to us. And still, this family is living in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did that take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because they like each other. I know that my mother-- first, it was big tolerance. Because my mother always said, she was not special. I can't tell that my mother was high educated. But she had heart. And she said, God is the same-- one God for everybody. And she likes if somebody was friend, not because of religion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was friends with Mrs. Melamed. They like each other, and my father because of business, and children because of age. They have two boys in age-- my age-- and one beautiful daughter a little younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that friendship come about with the Melameds?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think before the war, bef-- sure, before the war. I remember when my mother was thinking [NON-ENGLISH] where they had wholesale of some, some articles which my mother needed to the store. And we went together. Mrs. Melamed, always ask me, [? Paulinka-- ?] my middle name, my middle name. And even after 50 years, she's asking about me.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.
